posts.title: Wine Ordering: A Guide for the Confident Host
posts.body:
I consder my self one of the most fortunate and blessed people I know. I truly love my job. Working in risk management comes with a range of perks, one of which is the privilege of dining and sharing great wine with colleagues, clients, and friends. Whether at industry events or intimate dinners, there is something undeniably special about bonding over a great meal and an impressive bottle of wine. Over the years more people than I can count have asked me to put something like this together. So here it is.

## Why Wine

For me wine is not about chasing a buzz. If that is your goal, save yourself the time and money and grab a bottle of Jack. There is a unique romance about wine that no other drink can replicate. It has the power to bring food and people together in a way that is hard to explain until you experience it. Most of you know I am notorious for my four and five hour dinners. Wine is how those happen. It naturally pairs with a meal, enhances the experience, and sparks the kind of conversation you never want to end.

Wine is also wonderfully diverse, much like we are as people. It comes in a variety of styles from different regions and backgrounds, and it evolves over time. A bottle can taste completely different from one day to the next. Even your mood can influence how it lands on the palate. And of course, it simply tastes great.

I would not call myself a wine expert. Well versed is probably the right description. There are plenty of professionals who could run laps around my knowledge. But through the years I have developed some simple strategies for picking and ordering wine at restaurants that can help even the most casual wine drinker feel confident at the table.

## Who Is This Guide For

This is for anyone who dines at restaurants where wine is a featured part of the experience. Anyone who enjoys wine but does not necessarily consider themselves an expert. Anyone who finds themselves as the host at a dinner, whether entertaining clients, celebrating with loved ones, or just being that brave soul at the table willing to pick up the wine list when everyone else looks the other way. And honestly, sometimes it is just nice to impress the people you care about.

## Build Your Personal Wine Journal

Before we even get to the restaurant, start here. Every time you enjoy a wine worth remembering, take note of it. Grab your phone, snap a photo of the label, jot down a few words about what you liked. There are apps built specifically for this but the notes app on your phone works just fine. Here is what to look for:

The grape variety. Is it a Pinot Noir, Cabernet Sauvignon, Chardonnay, or something else? If it is a blend or the label is unclear, check the back label for the breakdown. For foreign wines that are harder to decode, ask your server or just Google it.

Where it is from. Napa, Sonoma, Washington, France, Italy, wherever. If it is not immediately clear, ask or look it up.

The sub-region. Go a level deeper. If it is from Sonoma, is it Alexander Valley or Russian River Valley? If it is Napa, is it Stags Leap, Oakville, or St. Helena? The more detail you have, the more informed your choices become over time.

The vintage. Check the year on the bottle to understand its age.

The price. Most restaurants list it. If not, look it up later. This helps you calibrate quality against cost over time.

The more you practice this the more intuitive it becomes. You are building a memory bank that pays dividends every time you sit down with a wine list.

## The Moment of Truth

You know the scene. Everyone is seated, menus have been handed out, and then the wine list lands on the table. More often than not guests react as if a grenade has been placed in front of them. Some freeze. Some look away. As the host or that brave soul willing to take charge, you pick it up. Now what?

First, gauge the table. Ask directly if everyone plans to drink wine. If two or more people are in, and you expect at least a glass or two each, ordering a bottle is almost always the better choice. Most restaurants offer wine by the glass but the selection is limited and the cost per glass is typically higher than splitting a bottle. A bottle also adds a shared experience that a round of individual glasses just does not replicate.

Second, keep the momentum going while you decide. Suggest cocktails, beer, or a glass of something simple to keep guests satisfied while you study the list. From the moment you receive the wine list to the first glass being poured can easily be ten to twenty minutes. Don't let guests sit empty handed.

Third, discover your group's preferences. Red or white is usually the first question. You will find most tables lean red but some guests have firm preferences. If one or two people insist on something specific let them order by the glass while you focus on a bottle that works for the majority. This is where your wine journal memory bank becomes your secret weapon. Look at the list and recall what you have enjoyed, what regions you loved, what styles resonated with you. If you once fell in love with a Pinot Noir from Oregon's Willamette Valley, look for something from that area. You are using your own experience as a guide, not trying to become an expert overnight.

## A Few More Things Worth Knowing

Choose restaurants known for well-curated wine lists. Fine dining spots are generally a safe bet and the staff will be more equipped to help you navigate.

Pairings are flexible. While matching wine to food perfectly is ideal it is not a hard rule. Red with fish, white with steak, it is all fine as long as the experience is enjoyable. If your table loves red but you are starting with seafood, a lighter red like a Pinot Noir from Oregon or Santa Barbara is a beautiful bridge between both worlds.

Budget wisely. In most fine dining restaurants quality red wines typically range from $100 to $200 and good whites from $50 to $100. You do not need to break the bank to enjoy something special.

Old World (Europe) wines are incredible but can be intimidating if you are not familiar with them. European labels often do not list the grape variety because you are expected to know the region. If you venture there and are not sure, own it. Tell your guests you are living on the wild side and enjoy the ride.

Lean on the experts. If you are stumped, ask the sommelier or a knowledgeable server. Share your past favorites, preferred regions, and budget. If you are concerned about price, simply point to a number on the wine list you are comfortable with rather than saying the amount out loud. It saves the awkwardness and gets you exactly where you need to be.

## Pro Tips

If you arrive early and have the opportunity to sit at the bar, this is your power move. Pick your bottle, have it opened right there, and let it breathe before your guests even sit down. The wine will be ready to drink the moment everyone is seated, you skip the pressure of choosing under the spotlight, and your guests will think it is the most effortless, baller move they have ever seen at a dinner. It absolutely is.

Most restaurants publish their wine list on their website. If you have ten minutes on the Uber ride over, take a quick look. You will arrive with a head start, a shortlist in your mind, and a confidence that the table will notice immediately.

Most restaurant wines are younger than the ideal age they should be consumed at. If you spend $100 or more on a bottle of red and it is not blowing you away on the first pour, do not hesitate to ask the server to have it decanted. Decanting opens the wine up and can transform it completely within twenty to thirty minutes. One thing to keep in mind though, if someone at the table is not having a cocktail and is literally sitting there waiting for the bottle, have a small amount poured for them right away so they have something to sip on while the wine opens up. Taking care of your guests is always the move.

## In Closing

There are very few right or wrong answers when it comes to wine. The process is as personal as it is enjoyable. Knowing how to choose and order a bottle with confidence does not require an encyclopedia of knowledge. It requires curiosity, a little practice, and the willingness to enjoy the journey.

The goal was never to make you an expert overnight. The goal is to make sure that the next time that wine list lands on the table, you pick it up with confidence, take care of your people, and let the evening do the rest.

Cheers. And as always, Journey Wine With Us. 🍷

posts.title: Santa Barabara Wine Country Itinerary - Kazmi Version
posts.body:
You asked for it. Over the years Rachel and I have sent this itinerary to more friends and colleagues than we can count. Every single one of them has come back saying the same thing: why did we wait so long to do this? So here it is, our two day Santa Barbara wine country guide, built from years of personal experience, refined trip after trip, and shared with nothing but love. Keep in mind this is just one example there are tons of other ways we can create this but this would be at the top of the list.

Fair warning. I am sending you to some of the finest wineries in the entire region. The tours are on the pricier side but every single penny is worth it. There are over two hundred wineries in Santa Barbara County so feel free to explore and discover your own way. That is honestly how we found all of these places. But if you want a head start, this is ours.

One practical note before we dive in. Once you are out in wine country it gets remote quickly. Keep snacks in the car, pack some water, and do not count on finding a restaurant around every corner. The wineries we are sending you to are worth every mile of winding road to get there.

## Where to Stay

Our top pick is the Fess Parker Inn in Los Olivos. It puts you right in the heart of everything, walking distance to tasting rooms, restaurants, and the charm of Los Olivos itself. It is our home base every time.

If pricing or preference takes you elsewhere, the Corque Hotel in downtown Solvang is a beautiful option. Walking distance to restaurants, shops, and some tasting rooms, though the tasting rooms in Solvang are not as strong as Los Olivos. Still a great base if you want that Danish village feel.

The Santa Ynez Valley Marriott in Buellton is where I usually end up because of the Bonvoy points and the central location. Very basic compared to the others but it works perfectly and puts you close to everything. Not as walkable though so keep that in mind.

Whatever you choose, make sure you hit both Los Olivos and Solvang during your trip. They are very different experiences and both worth your time.

## Day 1

Start your morning with breakfast at the Fess Parker Inn. Solid restaurant, great way to ease into the day before a big one.

Your first stop is Starlane. They have a tasting room called Dierberg but I strongly recommend booking the private tour at the actual Starlane property. The facility is absolutely gorgeous, the experience is magical, and the wines are exceptional. Great Cabs and Pinots. This is one of the two best wineries in the entire area in my opinion.

From there head to Crown Point. Book the tour here as well. Smaller facility but equally stunning and they make the best Cabernets in the entire Santa Barbara region. Keep in mind they are exclusively Cabs and Cab blends, all Bordeaux style, so if that is your thing this is your place. You can add a cheese pairing to keep you fed between tastings. These two wineries are close to each other and together they make for an unforgettable morning.

Head back into Los Olivos for lunch at Panino, delicious sandwiches and salads, exactly what you need to reset before the afternoon. After lunch hit one or two tasting rooms in town, they are all within walking distance of each other. I would make sure Dragonette is on the list, great Pinots and Rhône blends and our oldest standing wine membership in the area.

End the day with dinner at Hitching Post 2 in Buellton. Grab a cab or Uber there, it is not far, and plan on taking one back because you are going to want to enjoy yourself. Mesquite grill, order the steak with a side of quail or duck, yes I said side of. Thank me later.

## Day 2

Head to Solvang early and have breakfast at Paula's Pancakes or Ellen's. Both are famous for their Danish pancakes, very simple places, very solid food. After breakfast walk around the town for a bit. Solvang is a Danish themed village and it is genuinely charming, great bakeries, great shops, and the kind of place that makes you feel like you have been transported somewhere in Europe.

From Solvang make your way to The Hilt in Lompoc. It is a bit of a trek but absolutely worth it. Gorgeous property, stunning wines, red blends and Pinots that will stop you in your tracks.

After The Hilt head to Presqu'ile in Santa Maria, about thirty minutes away. Presqu'ile has an exceptional kitchen so you can taste and eat a proper lunch all in one place. The property is also stunning so book the tour if you can. This is a gem that a lot of people miss and it is not a mistake you want to make.

Make your way back to Los Olivos in the afternoon and hit one or two more tasting rooms. Stolpman is a must, amazing Rhône blends and some of the best whites in the region. Then walk to dinner at Mattie's Tavern, right there in Los Olivos, easy and wonderful.

## More Wineries Worth Knowing

These did not make the two day itinerary but they absolutely deserve a mention. If you have extra time or want to swap something out these are all places we love.

Foley — great Pinots and Chardonnays. Lincourt — great Pinots. Alma Rosa — great Pinots. Firestone — solid Rhône blends. Melville — great Syrah. Dovecote — great Rhône reds and whites. Brave and Maiden — gorgeous facility, sharp whites and excellent Rhône blends. Tyler — private tour at the home, fantastic Chardonnay, Pinots and Cabs.

## Restaurants Worth Knowing

There is no shortage of great food in the area but keep in mind once you are out in wine country it gets remote. Plan ahead, keep snacks in the car, and use this list as your guide.

For dinner and lunch: Hitching Post 2 in Buellton is a must and a staple in the area. Great food, not fancy, good old school vibe but high quality meats and seafood. My favorite restaurant in the entire area. SY Kitchen in Santa Ynez is a bit out of the way but worth the trek if you are up for it. Mattie's Tavern in Los Olivos is walking distance from the Fess Parker Inn, awesome food, set in a beautiful old inn. Tavern at Zaca Creek in Buellton is our newest favorite. Gathering Table in Solvang, walking distance from the Corque Hotel, Indonesian inspired fine dining that is genuinely special. Firestone Brewery for great beer and solid food. Industrial Eats is a real local gem, upscale food with a completely chill vibe. Panino for lunch, Big Bopper Drive-In if you want a greasy walk up burger that hits exactly right after a long day of tasting.

For breakfast: Ellen's, Paula's Pancakes, or any of the twenty little Danish cafes scattered around Solvang. Good Seed Coffee Boutique is a must and you will want to take some home. Enjoy Bagels and Sweets is another great morning stop.

## Getting Around

During the day, driving between wineries is perfectly fine especially if you are only hitting two or three stops and pacing yourself. At night is a different story. I would not drive after dark unless you have a rock solid designated driver.

For car service I use Golden Limo for my events and they are excellent. For taxis, Uber can be hard to find after 8 or 9 PM in wine country. 805 Taxi is reliable and you can schedule a ride ahead of time which I highly recommend doing before dinner so you are not scrambling at the end of the night.

## Pro Tips

If you decide to venture out on your own beyond this itinerary, map out your wineries before you go. The two day plan I put together keeps geography in mind for a reason. Some wineries in Santa Barbara County can be an hour apart from each other. A little planning saves you a lot of windshield time and keeps the day flowing.

When you book your appointments give yourself plenty of breathing room. There is no need to rush any of this. If you are booking a tour or doing a meal with your tasting, plan on a minimum of an hour and a half to two hours at each winery. These experiences are meant to be savored, not rushed through.

Always check the days each winery is open before you build your schedule. A lot of wineries in Santa Barbara are not open seven days a week and some are only open three or four days. Nothing worse than driving out to a gorgeous property and finding a locked gate. Check the website, call ahead, and confirm before you go.

## One Last Thing

Make reservations for everything well in advance. Most tasting rooms take walk ins but the four main wineries I recommended may not, especially on weekends. Do not leave that to chance.

If you want something more specific to your tastes, your group, or your budget, reach out!. I mean it. Rachel and I have been doing this for a long time and we are happy to help you build something personal. Honestly, putting this together makes me want to crash your trip every single time.

Either way you are going to have the best time. Santa Barbara wine country does not miss. 🍷
